content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• ) 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
0 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Christopher Drop 
General Manager 
Manila Community Services District 
1901 Park Street 
Arcata, CA 95521 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-281 
Dear Mr. Drop: 
January 10, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in governmental decisions relating to a project to 
build a playground structure at the Redwood Coast Montessori Charter School, including decisions 
relating to a United States Department of Agriculture grant to help fund the project, given that the 
playground structure would be built within 300 feet of your residence? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your 
residence distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18 I 10 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-281 
Page No. 2 
Manila is a small unincorporated community within Humboldt County, located on the 
Samoa Peninsula on the western edge of Arcata Bay. According to the United States Census 2010, 
Manilla encompasses an area of. 704 square miles, its population was 784 people, and there were 
411 housing units within the community. The Manilla Community Service District provides water, 
sewer, and park management services on behalf of the community. You are the District's General 
Manager. 
The District leases real property to the Redwood Coast Montessori Charter School, and the 
Charter School uses that leased real property as its school grounds. The Charter School has obtained 
a permit from the County for a project to build a playground structure on those grounds, and the 
proposed playground structure would be within 300 feet of your residence. 
Your residence is just north of the school grounds, and shares a property line with the school 
grounds. There is a large fence and wooded area between your residence and the school grounds. 
You note that an individual can access the school grounds from your property by foot by walking to 
the west, around the fence, and using a beach trail. The Charter School is located on Peninsula 
Drive, and your residence is located on an unpaved road off of Peninsula Drive; in order to access 
the school grounds by vehicle, you must drive east on the unpaved road and then south on Peninsula 
Drive to the entrance of the school grounds. 
The Charter School is currently raising funds and pursuing grants to help fund the project. 
One of the grants that the Charter School identified as a potential funding source for the project is a 
United States Department of Agriculture grant. However, only the owner of the real property 
associated with the grant application, in this case the District, may apply for that grant. 
The District will soon consider whether to approve the application for the USDA grant. You 
have asked whether the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in 
decisions relating to the project, including the District's consideration of the USDA grant 
application, because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on your real property interest in your residence. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on one or more of the official's financial interests. {Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official has a 
financial interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Therefore, assuming you have a direct or indirect interest of 
$2,000 or more in your residence, you have a real property interest in your residence. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-17-281 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's 
financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable. Subdivision (a) of that 
regulation provides that a financial interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is a 
named party in or the subject of the decision, and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if it 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. If the official's financial interest is explicitly involved 
in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on the official's interest is presumed 
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision 
at issue, Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the decision's financial effect is reasonably foreseeable 
if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Because your real property interest in your residence is not explicitly involved in the 
decisions relating to the project, those decisions' financial effect on that interest is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.2(b) sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to an official's 
real property interest that is inexplicitly involved in the decisions at issue. Paragraph ( 11) of that 
regulation provides that the financial effect of a decision on an official's inexplicitly involved real 
property interest is material if the decision affects "real property value located within 500 feet of the 
property line of the official's real property." However, paragraph (11) also provides that "the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will 
be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property." 
Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that the potential installation of a 
proposed playground structure at the Charter School in close proximity to your residence would 
have any measurable impact on the value of your residence. Due to fencing, natural obstacles, and 
the orientation of nearby roads, your residence would not have advantageous access to the potential 
playground structure when compared to the other 411 households within the . 704 square mile 
community. Furthermore, there is no other indication that the project would have any measurable 
financial effect on your real property interest in your residence. Therefore, we find that the 
decisions relating to the potential project would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your real property interest in your residence. 
Public Generally Exception 
Even if the decisions relating to the proposed playground structure would have a 
disqualifying financial effect on your real property interest in your residence, the Act does not 
prohibit an official from taking part in a decision if the financial effect of the decision on the 
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the "public generally." (Section 
87103; Conners Advice Letter, No. 1-15-113.) Commonly referred to as the "public generally 
exception," Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the following general rule: 


File No. A-17-281 
Page No. 4 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
For purposes of this provision, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 
percent of (I) all businesses or nonprofit entities within the official's jurisdiction; (2) the real 
property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the jurisdiction; or all 
individuals within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) A "unique effect" on a public official's 
financial interest, as defined in Regulation l 8703(c), includes a disproportionate effect on any of 
the following: 
"(I) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity 
of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from 
the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
"( 4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from 
the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size 
when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets, or liabilities, or real property if 
the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." 
Manila's population is approximately 784 people, and there are approximately 411 housing 
units within the . 704 square mile community. As noted above, due to fencing, natural obstacles, and 
the orientation of nearby roads, your residence would not have advantageous access to the potential 
playground structure. Considering Manila's limited population and geographical size, it appears the 
decisions at issue would affect a significant segment of residential real properties within Manila, 
and that those decisions' financial effect on your residence would not be unique among those 
residential real properties that make up the significant segment. Therefore, even if the decisions at 
issue would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in 
your residence, the public generally exception would apply. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the potential playground structure project at the Charter School 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your 
real property interest in your residence distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 


File No. A-17-281 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916} 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
/PIIJ(l:r/;{tr 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if that decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on one or more of the official's financial interests. {Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official has a 
financial interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Therefore, assuming you have a direct or indirect interest of 
$2,000 or more in your residence, you have a real property interest in your residence.

Regulation 18701 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's 
financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable. Subdivision (a) of that 
regulation provides that a financial interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is a 
named party in or the subject of the decision, and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if it 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. If the official's financial interest is explicitly involved 
in the decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on the official's interest is presumed 
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision 
at issue, Regulation 18701 (b) provides that the decision's financial effect is reasonably foreseeable 
if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
Because your real property interest in your residence is not explicitly involved in the 
decisions relating to the project, those decisions' financial effect on that interest is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.2(b) sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to an official's 
real property interest that is inexplicitly involved in the decisions at issue. Paragraph ( 11) of that 
regulation provides that the financial effect of a decision on an official's inexplicitly involved real 
property interest is material if the decision affects "real property value located within 500 feet of the 
property line of the official's real property." However, paragraph (11) also provides that "the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under these 
circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will 
be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property." 
Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that the potential installation of a 
proposed playground structure at the Charter School in close proximity to your residence would 
have any measurable impact on the value of your residence. Due to fencing, natural obstacles, and 
the orientation of nearby roads, your residence would not have advantageous access to the potential 
playground structure when compared to the other 411 households within the . 704 square mile 
community. Furthermore, there is no other indication that the project would have any measurable 
financial effect on your real property interest in your residence. Therefore, we find that the 
decisions relating to the potential project would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your real property interest in your residence.

Even if the decisions relating to the proposed playground structure would have a 
disqualifying financial effect on your real property interest in your residence, the Act does not 
prohibit an official from taking part in a decision if the financial effect of the decision on the 
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the "public generally." (Section 
87103; Conners Advice Letter, No. 1-15-113.) Commonly referred to as the "public generally 
exception," Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the following general rule:  
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment." 
For purposes of this provision, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25 
percent of (I) all businesses or nonprofit entities within the official's jurisdiction; (2) the real 
property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the jurisdiction; or all 
individuals within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) A "unique effect" on a public official's 
financial interest, as defined in Regulation l 8703(c), includes a disproportionate effect on any of 
the following: 
"(I) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
"(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity 
of a project that is the subject of a decision. 
"(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from 
the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
"( 4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from 
the official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size 
when a decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
"(5) A person's income, investments, assets, or liabilities, or real property if 
the person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." 
Manila's population is approximately 784 people, and there are approximately 411 housing 
units within the . 704 square mile community. As noted above, due to fencing, natural obstacles, and 
the orientation of nearby roads, your residence would not have advantageous access to the potential 
playground structure. Considering Manila's limited population and geographical size, it appears the 
decisions at issue would affect a significant segment of residential real properties within Manila, 
and that those decisions' financial effect on your residence would not be unique among those 
residential real properties that make up the significant segment. Therefore, even if the decisions at 
issue would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in 
your residence, the public generally exception would apply. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the potential playground structure project at the Charter School 
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
